speaker: interviewer
text: I'm interviewing Dr. Bronia Hatfiel, 9th of May, 1995. And this is take four. Bronia, we were talking a few minutes ago about the time in Vienna just as you were being ousted from that apartment. The original owners came back and reclaimed the apartment. What happened to you then?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, then I rented a very large room with some widow, who had a very large apartment across the road from where, if you don't mind-- where Freud lived. I didn't know it at the time. Now there is a museum there, which I visited several times, just across the street-- across the street. And I had then-- I already had my degree. I had no apartment. I had no reason to stay in Vienna permanently. It was never my intention to stay in Vienna permanently. I really was only there to get my degree.

speaker: interviewer
text: As a medical practitioner?

speaker: interviewee
text: As a medical practitioner, as an MD. And at that stage, I already had it. It was-- we're talking about 19-- yes-- 1950-- well, yes, after I graduated, of course. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: So I rented this-- this big room. And I stayed there. And I traveled with friends to Italy and whatever. And at one stage, I went to Israel. I went to Israel to visit Alex. Who was Alex?

speaker: interviewee
text: Alex was my first love from Vienna, who left Vienna. He went to live in Israel. He was a great Zionist, great-- tremendous patriot. He was the most handsome guy, because, you know, he had a car in Vienna, which was unheard of. He was a chauffeur. He was a student. And when he-- he was sitting-- anyway, when he walked or drove, women used to stop in the middle of the street and stare at him. He was really, you know, Hollywood caliber, extremely-- extremely-- and the voice and the charm. Anyway, before I-- or while I lived in Vienna, I got a permit to Australia and an affidavit to the United States. And people were queueing up to get to the United States. They waited for years. Everybody wanted to go to America. Everybody wanted to go to America. I got this affidavit from a-- my grandmother's-- my maternal grandmother's sister, who lived in Philadelphia, that I never heard of. How she found me, I shall never know, because I never met her.

speaker: interviewer
text: So she sent me this affidavit. And she sent it to me in early '46, '47-- very early. And I remember that I wrote to her straight-- then I got the permit to Australia. My cousins, how they found me, I also don't know. I think it was Joint-- American Joint Distribution. I think that-- So you had cousins in Australia?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, the only cousins that survived from Poland, the war, is from maternal side, was that cousin who survived in Siberia and finished up in the United States. And I'll explain to you in a minute how he got there. And these cousins, who are from father's side, who lived in Lublin-- he was an editor. The husband of my cousin was a very famous editor of a Polish newspaper. They had a car or a truck. I don't know what they had. They ran from Lublin. And they got through the frontier from Vilnius, Lithuania, through that-- I don't know whether you remember. There was a Japanese fellow, consul, who gave-- who saved thousands of Jews. His photo is hanging in the museum. They got their-- permission to leave Lithuania to Japan through this guy in that group of people. This Japanese fellow gave documentation and permission to-- so through the Trans-Siberian Railway, they got to Japan. And from Japan, by ship, they got to Australia. So they got here already in the '40s-- '48 or-- I don't know what year, or maybe even before-- probably before-- early '40s.

speaker: interviewer
text: So they were here. And they found out that I'm alive. So already-- They were in Kobe for a year, or whatever it is, so since '41, '42. They would have been here in '42, '43-- already here in Sydney.

speaker: interviewee
text: Anyway, they found me in Vienna, and they sent me this Australian permit. And I knew them, you see. As a child, I remembered them. These were the only relatives I really remembered. And they were alive. The one in America, I never heard of. I never knew them.

speaker: interviewer
text: So what did I do? I tore up the affidavit to the United States. I wrote to the auntie. Would you, please-- I am not going to use the affidavit, but would you send one to Sam? That was that cousin who came from Poland with me to Vienna, to Austria, and who finished up in the camp. Because he went to DP camp-- Displaced Persons camp in Salzburg.

speaker: interviewee
text: That was his choice. I wanted him to stay in Vienna. But he liked to be surrounded, you know, by-- I don't-- DPs. I don't know. He wasn't comfortable in Vienna. It was not his cup of tea. I had my girlfriends there. He had no one. But he was staying-- anyway, he chose himself. So I said to her, send him one. She sent him an affidavit, and he went to the United States. And he lived there until, you know-- until he died. He left two children. I mentioned that before. And one lives in Toronto, one in Washington.

speaker: interviewer
text: Now what was your question again? How did I get to Australia? Because of my-- these cousins, I came to Australia because of them. And I stayed with them for about three months. And then I lived in the rooms. I had landladies. I was studying.

speaker: interviewee
text: There was a family conference. I already came. I was 22 and 1/2 years of age-- 23. I had an MD Vienna in my pocket. I was very healthy. I spoke 15 languages. I didn't speak any English, but within a few days-- I used to take French magazines with me to the lectures here, to the Sydney University, for the first week. And then I started hearing, understanding.

speaker: interviewer
text: And when-- I enrolled in January. And-- when is the course? In April, there was a written in pathology or some other-- and I got the-- I got the prize. It was a written paper. In any case, I am very good with languages. So why am I telling you this? I-- I had all-- I was energetic. I was capable. I was an MD at 23, and the world was at my feet. I come to Australia. What do I do? I have to study again. I wanted to live a little. I wanted to have a bit of joy. I wanted to have a bit of fun.

speaker: interviewee
text: My family decided that I have to-- without me being present. They kicked me out of the room. I had to wait in the garden. They decided I should study. I studied for three years-- very hard. It wasn't easy in Australia, because in Australia, the method was different. You didn't discuss the patient with anybody. It was the tutor who discussed the organs of the patients in front of the patient. But the exams were very hard. I studied very hard for three years in Australia. Then I graduated here. I worked in-- because I was a foreign practitioner, I couldn't compete about residence in teaching hospitals. But eventually, I got to the Children's Hospital. I went to the Griffith's Hospital, first as a GP-- Children's Hospital. Then I was in private practice. I never wanted to settle down in private practice. I helped a very lovely doctor in Eastern Suburbs. I was his assistant for some time. And I decided I've had Australia, and I left Australia for two years to travel around the world.

speaker: interviewer
text: When was that?

speaker: interviewee
text: That was 1956. I graduated at the end of '53. I came in December 1950. I was one of the-- of course, I passed brilliantly. I never failed. I never had to repeat anything, because of my brain.

speaker: interviewer
text: So in any case, I graduated in-- the end of '53, registered in '54-- January '54. And in '56, I left Australia. I did all these residences and the private work in Eastern Suburbs.

speaker: interviewee
text: And then I left. I saved a little bit of money, because I was assisting in private practice. And I went around the world for two-- it lasted-- it lasted two years. I came back in '58. I went to Europe. I went to Vienna. I got engaged and disengaged. That's besides the point. I worked in London. I went to Argentina. My uncle summoned me to Argentina. You know, he sent me ticket. Pick-- I got a telegram in London-- pick up your ticket. So I had to pick up my ticket to travel to Buenos Aires. I didn't mind, but I didn't like my uncle particularly, you know, and his daughter even less.

speaker: interviewer
text: So I-- and they wanted me to study again in Argentina so that I stay in Argentina, you see. In Argentina, I would have to start the medical course from the beginning to become a doctor there. So I said to myself, these fellows must be crazy. They want me to spend my life as a student? So anyway, I packed my bags up. I came back to Australia in '68-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] '58. What are we talking about? '58.

speaker: interviewee
text: '58. I got married in '61. I was married for 15 years to a very nice guy. I really picked well. Because when I came back, I came back by ship via Italy. I came back from Argentina by a French ship to Genoa, from Genoa, an Italian ship to Australia. My cousins were waiting, you know. It was-- the ship landed in Woolloomooloo They were so overjoyed to see me, my cousins, you know, that I'm back. They were so thrilled and came with a list. Ten guys were waiting for me-- Cedric Simons and this one and that one and this one and that one. Jesus.

speaker: interviewer
text: My husband was number 11, but I already met him. My husband I only met after I came back, right? I met him-- no, I met him six months before I married him. So if I were married in April '61-- I married him on the 16th of April, 1961. And I met him at the end of '60. I knew him about-- for six months or so.

speaker: interviewee
text: And we had 15 years together, which were-- very interesting. We traveled a lot every year. We lived in a beautiful house. We had everything, but he was not a good companion. His mother called him an oyster when he was born, and he remained an oyster. And oysters are not that easy to live with, because they don't talk, and they don't hear. So I lived-- I was very lonely in the marriage, because I didn't maintain my friendships anymore. I didn't need any friends, I thought.

speaker: interviewer
text: So I lapsed my friends-- girlfriends and, you know doctor friends-- because I had a husband now. I'm supposed to be satisfied. I've got a guy. He's supposed to be my mate.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I had no one to talk to because we didn't share anything. He played golf. He played this, you know. But he was the brightest guy I ever met. He-- he's got a faster brain than I have, and that's saying something-- very intelligent, very well-traveled, very well-read, a guy who has done so many courses at the WEA, whatever it's called-- you know, the-- very beautiful and full of integrity, honest, beautiful guy. But because I felt so lonely and desperately lonely, I had to leave him. We didn't go for any marriage guidance. That was a big mistake. I-- but then again, he had a very possessive mother. He was the youngest of three sons.

speaker: interviewer
text: His father was the famous Major Hatfield, who used to be on the radio on ABC every morning of the week conducting exercises, you know? When I came to Australia, he was on ABC every morning on the radio. The television didn't exist. Anyway, he was the youngest of the three sons, and the mother was determined to keep him. The father died, so she was clinging on. She called him [INAUDIBLE]. She called him [INAUDIBLE], girl's name or something. She was hanging onto him, OK?

speaker: interviewee
text: So-- and he was attached to her, because it was-- he was conditioned like that. Well, in the beginning, it was very difficult, but I somehow or other-- the balance was struck that he-- it was OK. It was OK. But eventually, it got worse. We became alienated somehow. I don't know how it happened, but we grew apart. I started to become very interested in spirituality. I became a mediator over 20 years ago. I became very interested in altered states of consciousness. He was not at all. He was very pragmatic, very realistic. He was a radiologist. And he had to see in order to believe. He had to touch, to see, particularly, in order to believe. He was one of these scientific brains that you have to-- you have to-- you know, you have to perceive it with your senses. There are no extrasensory perceptions, nothing like that.

speaker: interviewer
text: So we got alienated. So, you know, I was listening to tapes that he never wanted to listen to, that he felt were-- he ridiculed them because he didn't-- he was not interested in them. And I started meditating. He didn't understand that.

speaker: interviewee
text: Then I-- I left him in 1976. I went to live in France first. Then in France, I stayed with meditators in the Alps. Then I lived in Paris on my own in an apartment. Then I went to London. In London, I lived many times, because I did my degrees in London. I-- I worked in London on many occasions.

speaker: interviewer
text: Your postgraduate degrees?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, of course. I went to London many times. And even before I married, I lived in London and worked in London before-- from Australia, on this two-year trip around the world, I worked in London. In any case, so I lived basically in France, in England, and in Israel. Actually, I stopped on the way to Australia from London. I stopped in Israel, and people were so warm that I came to stay. I packed my bags and went to live in Israel. I lived there for nearly three years-- for three years, but in the meantime, I came to Australia from Israel for six months, bought apartments here and stuff like that. To cut the long story short, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I stayed in Australia. I came back to Australia in 1981. In 1981, I opened a practice Macquarie Street. I led a wonderful life. I had very close friends here-- new friends, completely new friends, not the ones that I had when I was married. The people I had that I-- when I was married fell by the wayside completely. I didn't maintain it with them. They didn't ring me. I didn't ring them. And I had created a new life for myself with wonderful friends. Unfortunately, they all died. The three closest ones died. The woman called Gisella-- you probably knew her. Anyway, a beautiful lady. She was old. All these people were old. My friends were old. She was in her 80s when she died. She was 86 when she died. Then another friend died. He was in his 70s, middle 70s. He died. And then somebody else died-- three of them. Oh, no, the third one didn't die-- no. He pushed me into marrying him. He courted me for 11 years-- a professor, actually-- a Jewish guy. Courted me for 11 years until about three years ago. And then, ultimatum he gave me-- either I marry him, or else. I said, I don't want to marry you. I'm quite comfortable as a friend with you, but I don't want to marry you.

speaker: interviewer
text: And he wanted marriage, you see. He wanted to be married. He wanted to be looked after by a woman, right? So he eventually finished up marrying a woman in Melbourne. Tell us more about your spiritual research that you've been working on.

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, after I began to meditate-- I found that I was getting states of consciousness which were making me happy, which put me in touch with myself, made me feel a part of the universe every time, just about, without fail. I felt-- I felt full of life, full of ability, full of integrity. I felt that I was-- I was a part of the universe. And I didn't need anybody to tell me things. I didn't need anybody to lean on. I was so strong and so happy that I felt other people should know about this.

speaker: interviewer
text: So I went yearly-- every year-- that was after I divorced Ken. Every-- his name was Ken Hatfield, Kenneth Hatfield. After I divorced Ken, every year-- after I came back to Australia, from '81-- from '81. I went overseas to conferences, international world conferences in psychiatry. And I alerted psychiatrists to these levels of consciousness that they were not-- that were not taught in psychiatry.

speaker: interviewee
text: It was totally missed. They never-- psychiatrists never discovered them. Freud didn't know about them. Jung knew about them. Freud didn't know about them. And Jung only knew about it because a friend of his lived in China and had to do with Confucius-- and it was the Chinese-- and wrote a book called 'The Golden Flower,' which I've got here. Somebody called Richard, he name is, you know. And Jung-- Jung wrote the introduction to the book. And that's how-- and Jung was warning the Western world, saying, don't mix with the East. It's dangerous. Jung didn't really, properly understand that there's no danger, that the soul of the Eastern person and Western person is the same, that only the conditioning and the superficial layers that are added by the environment are different. The customs, the traditions are different, but the functioning of the human being is the same. In any case, after I discovered that, I-- I was talking at the-- and I became very well known internationally-- very well-known-- among psychiatrists, of course. I avoided it in Australia. In Australia, I only made a couple of speeches, and that was all. Somehow or other-- I don't know. Maybe I'm a coward. Maybe I didn't want-- I don't know. Or maybe I thought it wasn't worth my effort here, because it used to be such a provincial place here. By the way, when I got to Australia, I got a culture shock. It was terrible. The first year-- if I-- look, first of all, if I survived Australia without getting undue nervousness or needing some treatment, it's a miracle, because the culture clash from Vienna-- I travelled a lot from Vienna, you see. I went to France with the students, the Jewish students. We had trips to Vienna. I went with my friends to Italy. I was a woman of the world.

speaker: interviewer
text: I come to Australia-- boy, all the fellows, the students at the university-- you know what they talked about? They talked about the races. They talked about girls, and beer.

speaker: interviewee
text: And if you read a book or two, which were not medical-- very few discussed things which are not medical. Maybe they played cards in the interval between the lectures-- stupid cards. It was just so barren. It was a shock to my system, because my friends in Vienna at least were interested in literature-- were all intellects, you see. No spirituality very much in Vienna. The spirituality, don't forget, I discovered from Australia-- in Australia, not in-- maybe had I stayed in Vienna, like my friends who I visit in Vienna, they know nothing of the things I know about-- spiritual life. They're still beautiful people. They worship the intellect. They-- they've got their families. They've got their children, grandchildren. They have beautiful lives. Vienna is-- now life in Vienna is-- you can't imagine how beautiful it is-- beautiful, absolutely beautiful. I was there not so long ago.

speaker: interviewer
text: But the life that I led, they've got no idea. I've never told them. They wouldn't suspect that there's another way of living that they've never even discovered. you know? And the way of living that I'm talking about now is the spiritual life.

speaker: interviewee
text: I made it my business to explore a lot of meditation groups. I went to the Tibetan Buddhists. I went to the other Buddhists. I went to the Hindus. I went-- I started with the TM. That was my first-- Maharishi Mahesh Yogi. My biggest roots are in the TM movement, but I'm not dependent on them. I do help them, I must say. From time to time, I do help them. I went in January to Melbourne to meditate with them. I felt I need a holiday. And they do nothing-- just meditate all day and have healthy vegetarian food, which was marvelous.

speaker: interviewer
text: So I used to do a lot of retreats. I used to go to the Hindus in the Siddha yoga in Ashfield-- not Ashfield-- [INAUDIBLE] Hill. I made it my business to explore how come-- I wasn't going to stick to one group. I wanted to know how the others are growing.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I realized it doesn't matter what type of meditation you use. As long as it agrees with you, you'll grow. I saw the people grow like nobody's business, regardless which group they belonged to. They were growing in a sense that they took no nonsense. Their sense of values changed. They didn't go for petty gossip. They were looking for deeper meaning of life. These were intelligent people who couldn't live on the surface, just leading-- in Poland, they used to call them the bread eaters. They were not only bread eaters. They needed more. These people, who took up meditation in Australia and around the world, needed a meaning in their life. They wanted to know, you know, more than just what-- what we were taught so far. And in Australia, now you've got people, you know, who branched out from all religions and swapped over. But I am not in favor of swapping over one religion for another. If it suits them, good luck to them. We are free citizens. This is the beauty of this country here. I went last night to see dancing by gays in the government opera house. And they were worshipping the gay relationships. Australia allows that. Other countries don't. We don't-- I don't know whether we appreciate what we have here. We have freedom. We can say anything we like. We can do-- as long as we don't hurt anybody, we can do what we like in this country-- what a gem of a place-- what a gem of a place.

speaker: interviewer
text: Have you found any solace or comfort in Judaism? Have you been able to--

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, you see, this created a conflict. When I started meditating, TM, which is based on Hinduism, all of sudden, I discovered wisdom like I never had before in my life. Because it didn't go with the Jewish religion. The Bible, in my opinion, doesn't give you that wisdom. The Bible is full of-- well, I don't know. To me, the Bible is full of violence, full of inconsistencies. I never read it properly, but what I have read, which is parts of it, were full of violence. And I didn't like-- I didn't like parts of it. To me, I don't know who wrote the Bible. I don't know which god gave it to Moses. I don't know-- I don't know. I don't take it very seriously. I discovered some truths, which came from inside and came probably partly from Judaism, from Christianity. Don't forget that I had a tremendous Christian influence on me because I went to Polish schools. Don't forget, at a very sensitive age-- the most sensitive age-- from the age of about five or six, to-- until the Soviets came in '39, I was surrounded by Jesus Christ. When I went to school, to the high school, the Jewish kids-- every morning, there was a prayer at 8 o'clock. And the Jewish kids stood, because we didn't kneel down. So from 8 o'clock in the morning, we knew we were different. I didn't kneel to Jesus Christ. I wasn't born Christian. On the other hand, I didn't believe in the Bible, either. When my father went to-- to the synagogue-- I went only once with him as a child. He took me and had me on his knees-- and Lola-- only once. So we didn't go to shul, and we didn't go to church. But influence-- the Christian influence-- was much stronger on me than the Jewish one. The Jewish one, I had at home. The Christian one, I had at school. And I was more at school, having more contact at school with the people, than I had at home. At home, we had meals, and they went their own way, and I went my own way. At school, we were all in it together. To answer your question, I freed myself-- I was always free from religious influences. Somehow or other, it didn't sit well with me-- never-- never. On the other hand, the communist ideas I liked, but I never joined any party. There was some kind of premonition-- I knew that they were lying. They used to tell me in Kiev that the Polish children starved. There was no sugar in Poland.

speaker: interviewer
text: I used to say to them, you're telling me there was no sugar in Poland? I was born in Poland. We had everything. You don't have anything. You see, I was so young, I could tell them that, because they wouldn't have put me to jail. I could tell the Russians what I thought.

speaker: interviewee
text: But what I'm telling you is that I wasn't very easily influenced by anybody. I was resistant. Somehow there's a core in me that resists-- I didn't belong to any Jewish organizations, either. I've never belonged-- now I belong to the spiritual ones. I belong to this one. I belong to Conflict Resolution. I belong to, you know, quite a-- quite-- quite a number of them, a lot of them.

speaker: interviewer
text: But not Jewish organizations?

speaker: interviewee
text: Not Jewish. Oh, well, I belong to one Jewish organization that determines who should get what. And I'm not a businesswoman. They're weren't going to keep me there very long.

speaker: interviewer
text: It's the Jewish-- what is it called? Foundation of the Jewish Welfare Services or something like that. The biggest businessmen of Sydney belonged to it and I. What am I doing there? I don't know. Bronia, do you have one message that you'd like to give for future generations?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, the only thing-- the one that I do belong to is the Jews that don't believe-- it's called the Jewish-- [PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] yes, I am a foundation member of the Jewish Secular Humanist Community. This is a group of Jews who believe that what you were born, you may as well stick to the traditions. You may as well stick to the traditions that you were born into, but if you don't want to believe in God, it's your privilege. Don't believe in God. Well, I am a member of that one. Now, my message to the future generations is-- [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] I think that the technique I applied is a good one. If an adversity strikes you in life, get on with life. Get over the adversity. Solve it the best way you know how, and get on with your own life. Get on contributing, just making things which are good for you and good for everybody else. And if you achieve that, then you would have achieved a lot. If you-- by doing that, you would serve humanity. Either you choose music or painting or medicine or law or whatever you may choose. As long as you-- you do it with full integrity and the best intention of helping and getting happy in the process yourself-- because this is the only way to get happy, is the gratification of knowing that you've done something worthwhile with your life, some work that involves other people, that's not just for you, that you survived the war not just for you.

speaker: interviewer
text: Why did I choose medicine? I wanted to fulfill some kind of a mission in the world. Because when I took up medicine in Kiev, the doctors didn't earn anything. They were earning less than the street cleaners. They shoved them. They sent them here and there and everywhere. And I never knew at the time that I would be leaving Russia. I didn't know that I'd be leaving Kiev. What did I know? I took up medicine because I wanted to justify my survival, that I survived and I-- I want to contribute to the welfare of humanity. To me, a life without an ideal is an empty life. Thank you. Can you tell us about this photo, Bronia?

speaker: interviewee
text: This is the photo of my mother, who was then Regina Nisman, before she married my father, Herman Goldstein. She would've been here-- I don't know-- about 20 years of age, or maybe 19. I have no idea. And it was given to me by someone-- I can't even remember who gave it to me. Because I had no-- I had no belongings, no photographs, when I left Lutsk at all. And someone gave it to me. And this is my mother.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you. We're not rolling.

speaker: interviewee
text: Sorry. This is my sister, Lola, and myself in Rabka, which is in the Polish mountains by Zakopane. And that morning I lost my little stick. So now I-- I have one hidden here in Australia. Because it was a sad day that day, yet I managed to smile. I lost that stick, and my sister has one, and I-- anyway, this is in Rabka before the war. Oh, before the war. I would have been-- I don't know-- four or five here. We were still living in Lublin. Oh, no, we were living in Lutsk already. I'm not sure-- probably Lublin-- Lublin still-- still Lublin.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. Tell us about this one.

speaker: interviewee
text: This is my sister, Lola. This is also when we were living in Lublin. The outfits, by the way, were sent by Uncle Adolf from Vienna. They are Tyrolean outfits, if you can notice, with the buttons and the little shorts-- Typical Tyrolean outfits from Vienna. My sister, Lola, my cousin, [INAUDIBLE] Goldstein. She was killed in Lutsk. He was killed in-- I don't know where-- probably in a camp near Lublin. This is myself. I'm the only survivor here. And [INAUDIBLE], another cousin. This is in Lublin. I would say around 1930.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you.

speaker: interviewee
text: This is my father's younger brother, Leon Goldstein, with his wife [INAUDIBLE]. They had a little daughter, [? Helinka ?]. They got killed in Sarni, in [INAUDIBLE], in Sarni, small place, not that far from Lutsk, but-- well, in the same area, more or less, but maybe-- I don't know-- 200 kilometers or what away from one another. They were killed there. Uncle Leon Goldstein, [INAUDIBLE]-- Auntie [INAUDIBLE], and [? Helinka. ?]

speaker: interviewer
text: And this was taken before the war?

speaker: interviewee
text: This was taken-- this was taken before the war. This was taken before-- this was probably taken after they got married, before even [? Helinka ?] was born. This is my sister, Lola, just before the war. She would have been-- in '39, she would have been 17 years of age. She was born on the 9th of July, 1922. This photograph was-- was another one, which will follow-- was carried by a fellow called [INAUDIBLE], who was a solicitor in Tel Aviv, in Israel. He gave me these photographs after the war on one of my visits to Israel-- or maybe when I lived there. And these are copies of his photographs, which he carried, as he told me, on his heart throughout his exile to Siberia, or Russia, somewhere in Russia, where he survived the war himself and then after came to Israel to live after the war. This is the other one. This is [INAUDIBLE] here with Lola, just before the war broke out. Because I remember the outfit-- this particular dress. It was just before the war broke out in 1939. And this photo with previous one survived on his bosom. The originals survived on the bosom somewhere inside Russia throughout the war. He gave it to me in Tel Aviv after the war. This is what I looked like when I arrived from Kiev to Vienna. I was plump. I lived on potatoes and bread, of course, in Kiev. There was nothing else-- and apples. And shortly after, I lost the weight and-- and I looked different.

speaker: interviewer
text: Oh, sorry. Yeah. OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: This is what I looked around-- when I was 30, 33. I don't know. Approximately in my 30s, that's what I looked like. This is the original mass grave with a sign in Lutsk of the 27,000 people that were killed that now have a marble monument on it. This was the original one. And these are the handful of people that I struck in Lutsk when I came on my second visit to check up on survivors, whether there were any left, from Kiev to Lutsk in 1945.

speaker: interviewer
text: Most of these people you see here survived either in the Soviet army or somewhere inside Russia. But two or three of them survived in the ghetto with me. What happened to them now? I don't know where they are. One lives in Buenos Aires.

speaker: interviewee
text: This article was written in a newspaper called [INAUDIBLE] in Lutsk, where I visited last year in August. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] When the editor of the newspaper [INAUDIBLE] found out I was in town, came to interview me. And I allowed-- I-- I permitted the interview under one condition, that she is going to publish in the paper that Jews lived in that city. Because when I went-- this castle, medieval castle, is now a museum. And when I, with my cousin Lara, I went to visit it, there was not a word about Jews having ever lived in Lutsk. This was very hurtful to me. And I agreed to this-- to this interview only under the condition that she will publish it in the paper. And indeed, she did. In this paper, she admits that there were 25,000 Jews-- or 27,000 Jews living in Lutsk, and that they were killed under the Germans, and the mass grave is still there. So this is the article admitting that Lutsk had Jewish inhabitants, whereas it was hidden. There is not a sign of it anywhere in Lutsk-- not a sign of it.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you.

speaker: interviewee
text: This is in Washington, DC, last year when my cousin Ben [? Weiger ?] married Fran-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Fran. And this is after the wedding. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] He is the son of the cousin who survived in Russia, with whom I left Poland to go to Austria, who went then under affidavits sent from the-- by the auntie from Philadelphia to the United States. So this is after the wedding of his son Benjamin in Washington, DC, 1994. Well, that's the story in a nutshell, except for adding that-- [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] one of my greatest passions in the later years now was the necessity for people to find themselves. And for this purpose, I organized a center for research in spiritual development, because I really know how important it is to feel good about oneself and about people in general. And if you would achieve that, that would really lead to peace in the world without playing games, politics, without having to resort to measures which are so inhuman, so ugly as the Nazi era has proven, that racism and hatred are so destructive that anything that will eradicate it is worthwhile living for.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you.
